Ready, aim, FOIA: Keeping up with the digital revolution

Bob Anderson Freedom of Information Center November 1996
Data & DatabasesInvestigative MethodsJournalism & Newsroom

This edition explores the challenges journalists face in accessing electronic data under the new Electronic FOIA Amendments. Contributors discuss the hurdles and legal interpretations that affect the availability of data, highlighting cases and examples from various states.

The Freedom of Information Center has donned electronic flak jackets and entered the digital revolution. After responding to questions about accessing government secrets for almost 40 years, the center is now firing back answers to a bundle of queries its founders never could have imagined. Some examples include questions about which states have the best open records laws for access to electronic information, whether federal government e-mail is considered a public document, and what states can charge for copies of electronic databases.

As access to electronic information becomes more feasible with laws like the Electronic FOIA Amendments, the role of the FOI Center continues to be crucial in providing researchers and journalists with the means to understand government information control.
